result:
A man wakes to the sound of a bus braking in the street below his apartment, and for three seconds he is convinced it is the ocean.

It’s not that he’s never known the sea. Once, when he was seven, his mother took him on a bus that really did end at a shoreline: a flat, gray English morning where the sky and water were almost the same color and the wind turned everyone’s hair to desperate flags. He remembers the taste of salt, the shriek of gulls, paper cones of hot chips that burned his fingers, and his mother laughing in a way he never saw again—head thrown back, shoulders loose, like a door had been unlatched in her.

The brakes squeal, the illusion breaks, and the apartment returns: the soft hum of the refrigerator, a neighbor’s radio thudding faintly through the wall, the distant elevator’s reluctant machinery. City sounds. Dry sounds.

He lies still another minute, letting the phantom sea linger. There is something about those first confused instants of waking that feels like standing at a border crossing between lives, passport not yet stamped. Choices still hypothetical. The day has not claimed him; his identity is not yet fully loaded. He feels like he could get up and be anybody.

He doesn’t. He gets up and is himself.

The kettle is old enough to have opinions. It clicks resentfully, buzzes in a pitch that he is nearly sure is complaining, then shudders toward a boil. While it works, he leans against the counter and looks at the magnets on the fridge: a cheap plastic Eiffel Tower, a ceramic avocado from a friend who believes in vitamins, a flat circular magnet with a picture of a galaxy and the words YOU ARE HERE printed cheerfully over a spiral arm that is not the one Earth actually inhabits.

He has known this for years and has never corrected it. It pleases him, absurdly, to imagine millions of identical magnets all lying about the universe’s architecture on stainless-steel doors.

The kettle snaps off. He pours the water over instant coffee—no grand rituals today, no scales or timers—and watches the dark granules bloom into a cloud and vanish. He has always enjoyed that small, dissolving moment, the way something seemingly solid is revealed to have been a powder waiting for heat and water and purpose. He wonders, not for the first time, how much of his own solidity is like that: habits and roles that look firm from a distance but would disperse if someone poured something hotter into his life.

The coffee is too hot and slightly bitter. He drinks it anyway.

He is forty-six. This feels like a compromise of an age, as if forty and fifty were arguing and the universe slid him down between them like a coaster between two glasses. He has a job involving spreadsheets and gentle emails, a plant whose continued survival constantly surprises him, and a collection of books he halfway believes he will read again. He has, more importantly, a habit of glancing at his phone every few minutes and finding nothing particularly urgent waiting for him.

He does not know exactly when urgency seeped out of his days and was replaced by this constant, polite background hum. Years ago, life had rhythms of exam dates, performance reviews, moving boxes, weddings, funerals, illnesses, promotions. Now it is a long, level road, pleasantly paved, with carefully spaced traffic lights. He understands that this is what many people would define as a good outcome. He is quietly grateful, and also—not unhappy, exactly, but tinged.

It is the tint that catches him as he stands in his small kitchen with its faux-marble counter and slightly uneven tiles. The knowledge that the day ahead could be swapped with almost any day from the last three years and he might not notice, except for the weather.

He opens his phone, intending to scroll, and for some reason, his thumb hesitates over the social media apps. It drifts instead to the notes icon. A list stares back at him:

– learn to bake bread  
– call Uncle Peter  
– fix dripping tap  
– book dentist  
– “write something? anything. 500 words. don’t care if it’s garbage”  

The last item has been there for months, the question mark and self-directed sarcasm solidified into a kind of ironic plaque.

He taps on it. Below, there is a date from half a year ago, and then twenty-seven words he doesn’t remember typing:

“The trouble with starting is that you must admit you have not already begun. Many people will die halfway through a sentence they never wrote.”

He reads the line twice. It feels like something left by a previous tenant of his brain, someone more earnest or more melodramatic. But there it is, his own phrasing, his own particular rhythm of clause and pause. He likes and dislikes it at the same time. It’s neatly turned and slightly too aware of being neatly turned.

Coffee in hand, phone in the other, he walks to the window. His view is of other windows and a narrow strip of sky. A pigeon, hunched and philosophical, occupies the neighboring building’s ledge, regarding the morning with the same cautious neutrality he feels. The bus that woke him has gone. Another will come.

The note’s cursor blinks, small, insistent. The app does not care whether he writes marketing copy, a diary, a confession, a joke. It offers only a space and a pulsing reminder that the space is not yet filled.

He thinks of the boy on the seaside bus. His mother clutching the plastic seatback, knuckles pale, determined not to be carsick in front of him. The smell of diesel and seaweed mixing into an oddly intoxicating promise.

“No one warned me,” his mother said then, “that life would be mostly… maintenance.” He had been seven, too young to understand, old enough to know it mattered. “You think it’s going to be… I don’t know. Fireworks. Trumpets. And it’s mostly remembering to defrost the chicken.”

He had laughed, because of the word “trumpets,” picturing their brass mouths opening and closing in disappointed silence over a bowl of frozen meat.

Later, much later, he would recall the way she’d looked out the window after saying it, as though embarrassed at having spoken aloud something she had meant to keep as an internal memo.

Maintenance. He looks now at his plant, its leaves glossy from the water he remembered to pour yesterday. At his laundry basket, not yet full enough to mandate action. At himself in the reflection of the dark television screen: a man with sleep-flattened hair, an old T-shirt, a cup, a phone, a galaxy magnet.

The word “maintenance” continues ringing somewhere behind his eyes, but it sounds different than it did when his mother wielded it. Then, it had been tinged with resignation, as if she were reporting from the front lines of disappointment. Now, it carries a quieter assertion: that keeping something going is not the opposite of living, but a component of it. That there is a strange dignity in not letting things crumble.

And yet. And yet.

He places the coffee on the windowsill, thumbs the cursor forward, and types:

“I am, today, choosing to start in the middle.”

He pauses. The sentence is not good, but it is honest. He remembers a piece of advice he once read: never begin at the beginning; it is the dullest place. Fine, then, he will cheat. He will assume that his life, unbeknownst to him, has already amassed a beginning, some invisible narrative coil behind him, and that this, now, is the middle, where something must at last occur.

He types again, filling the digital white:

“There will be no dramatic inciting incident: the world continues, more or less, as it was yesterday. No messenger arrives. No letter changes everything. A man wakes, mistakes a bus for the sea, makes coffee, and for no particular reason decides to write one true paragraph before lunch.”

He looks at what he has written. He hasn’t quite decided whether the “man” is definitively him or some fictional neighbor superimposed like tracing paper over his own outline. The ambiguity is comforting. He takes another drink. The coffee has cooled to something drinkable, if not especially pleasant.

On the sill, the light shifts. Morning leans a little more decisively into the room, illuminating dust motes that had previously been anonymous. They swirl in tiny galaxies, utterly indifferent to magnets and captions.

He writes:

“Nothing may come of this paragraph. The day may swallow it whole. Emails will accumulate, bills will arrive, buses will brake, potatoes will be boiled for dinner. Maintenance will continue its gentle empire. But for a brief interval, I will have arranged words in an order that did not exist yesterday, and this, absurdly small as it is, will be the closest thing to fireworks I am likely to manage on a Monday.”

He stops, surprised at a prickle at the back of his eyes. It’s not sadness, not exactly. More like the feeling you get when a song from years ago plays unexpectedly in a supermarket and somehow the fluorescent lights and the stacked cans of soup become, for four minutes, a place where your past self walks beside you, humming the melody.

He adds:

“If I am lucky, I will read this in ten years and wince. Wincing is proof of movement.”

The cursor blinks. Outside, someone shouts for a child to hurry. A siren in the distance climbs a scale and then disappears. The pigeon, having reached a decision about the day’s prospects, launches itself into the air with a soft explosion of wings.

His coffee is finished. The paragraph is, for the moment, enough.

He closes the notes app without re-reading more than once. On the fridge, the wrong galaxy continues to insist YOU ARE HERE, arrow pointing confidently to a place he is not. He decides, on a mild and unspectacular impulse, to leave it exactly where it is.

There is something appealing, he thinks, in the possibility that you can be honestly misplaced and still, somehow, precisely where your own story must begin again, and again, and again—each time in the unremarkable middle of an ordinary day, when a bus sounds like the sea.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
